tweets.text:
Trump's statements today on Saudi Arabia deserve more attention than they are getting, and a few things are worth keeping in mind.  1-Operation Epic Fury has two dimensions, one military and the other economic. Now that a ceasefire is in place, the economic campaign continues, and it's a very important one. I explain the economic damage on Iran's economy here:  https://t.co/isW7t9KnrS 

Destroying missile stockpiles produces battlefield results, but ensuring the IRGC cannot reconstitute those stockpiles produces structural damage, and that is precisely what the US is now pursuing through sanctions enforcement and loophole closure. The parallel sanctions pressure on China carries real weight as well, since it makes Beijing realize that helping Iran has costs. I wrote about it here: https://t.co/Pr9i0IjHzx   

2- On the Gulf, the question of whether the UAE and Saudi Arabia see eye to eye on every matter is, from a US strategic point of view, largely beside the point. What Washington needs is a region capable of guarding its own neighborhood, with Israel integrated into that architecture given its unmatched military capabilities.   I think that Trump is right now using this ceasefire moment to extract concessions from Riyadh that no previous administration managed to secure. Keep in mind that Saudi Arabia had been building a new coalition with Turkey and Pakistan to counterbalance the UAE and the Abraham Accords countries, which is a legitimate play. At a minimum, however, all of these actors share a common interest in containing Iran, there is already a lot to work with since Gulf countries are all working with CENTCOM and Israel, and that shared interest is the entire strategic outcome of what Operation Epic Fury was meant to produce.

tweets.text:
I genuinely feel like Darkloom should get 10x more attention than it has now.

Basically, the thesis behind it is that AI requests need compute to run, and most of that compute still comes from centralized cloud infra. So why not use millions of idle Apple Silicon Macs as part of a private AI inference network?

Instead of your Mac sitting unused after work or overnight, it could help process AI requests in the background.

I wanted to give it a go, so I tried their provider calculator with an M4 MacBook Pro, 48GB memory, Gemma 4 26B-A4B, and 16 inference hours per day.

The estimate came out to around $253/month after electricity costs, or about $3,031/year.

Money doesn’t grow on trees, so I’ll try to make it grow from my Mac instead lol.

Jokes aside, the side income part is cool, but I think the bigger picture is using idle Macs to make AI compute cheaper and more private.

If this works at scale, Darkbloom could become a new supply layer for AI inference, using existing hardware.

I'm looking forward to when they expand it to normal Windows PCs and phones, too. This could unironically be one of the biggest disruptors in the compute shortage we're experiencing worldwide.

Disclosure: I hold $EIGEN and have been a longtime @eigenlabs supporter.

I genuinely feel like Darkloom should get 10x more attention than it has now.

Basically, the thesis behind it is that AI requests need compute to run, and most of that compute still comes from centralized cloud infra. So why not use millions of idle Apple Silicon Macs as part of a private AI inference network?

Instead of your Mac sitting unused after work or overnight, it could help process AI requests in the background.

I wanted to give it a go, so I tried their provider calculator with an M4 MacBook Pro, 48GB memory, Gemma 4 26B-A4B, and 16 inference hours per day.

The estimate came out to around $253/month after electricity costs, or about $3,031/year.

Money doesn’t grow on trees, so I’ll try to make it grow from my Mac instead lol.

Jokes aside, the side income part is cool, but I think the bigger picture is using idle Macs to make AI compute cheaper and more private.

If this works at scale, Darkbloom could become a new supply layer for AI inference, using existing hardware.

I'm looking forward to when they expand it to normal Windows PCs and phones, too. This could unironically be one of the biggest disruptors in the compute shortage we're experiencing worldwide.

Disclosure: I hold $EIGEN and have been a longtime @eigenlabs supporter.

your criticisms of stablecoins are not new. you're recycling gorton and zhang's 2021 paper on stablecoins and NQA which has been taken up variously by brainard, krugman, and warren to undermine the sector. this attack is half a decade old. it relies on an ahistorical and myopic reading of free banking. not to mention a misreading of how stablecoins actually work.

i'm honestly tired of it. why don't you read dowd, kroszner, white, or selgin on free banking rather than parroting the historically illiterate fable of the period told among central bankers today? 

or, we could engage with the reality of stablecoins rather than resorting to analogy if you prefer. if you did, you would know that there is no comparison with the Reserve fund, which contained Lehman commercial paper, something that no GENIUS regulated stablecoin could hold. you would know that secondary market trading is not a reliable guide to its peg, when direct redemption is possible. 

if you really want to invoke free banking to undermine stablecoins I am more than happy to do that. but it doesn't support your argument in the slightest. it completely undermines it. you of all people should know that.

Incredibly excited to finally see the Transparency Alliance being revealed, and even more proud to see @MoonrockCapital alongside such strong industry players.

@JasonYanowitz and @MikeIppolito_ are among the very few people genuinely trying to fix this industry and make the pie grow again.

One of crypto’s biggest structural problems has undoubtedly been broken trust, combined with a lack of communication and transparency around token value accrual while regulatory clarity remains absent.

What @Blockworks pioneered with the Token Transparency Framework (TTF) and their work around investor relations, they are now taking to the next level with this Transparency Alliance.

I have little doubt that eventually issuing tokens as true digital equity onchain will become normal. If not, why are we even here?

Until we get there, trust and transparency are the best tools we have to bridge that gap.

And that is exactly what this Alliance is fighting for.

Blockworks is becoming crypto’s version of the SEC.

And the TTF is becoming the equivalent of an S-1 filing.

Pay attention to the founders filing with Blockworks, and do question those who don’t.

This will become the new standard that fixes the industry again.

F3 #Uranium $FUU recently dropped one of the most important updates yet for the Tetra Zone, and the biggest takeaway may not be the radioactivity itself, but the confirmed genetic link to the ultra-high-grade JR Zone.

The latest step-out drilling intersected uranium mineralization in multiple holes and confirmed that Tetra remains open along strike and down plunge. But the real game changer is the structural + geochronology work showing that Tetra and JR share:

✅ identical uraninite generations
✅ overlapping uranium age events
✅ the same hydrothermal system
✅ the same long-lived structural corridor

This is potentially huge.

F3 is essentially demonstrating that JR may not be an isolated ultra-high-grade occurrence, but part of a much larger district-scale uranium system extending across the PLN/Broach corridor.

Even more interesting:
Tetra is proving uranium mineralization can occur in “non-traditional” non-graphitic settings, dramatically expanding the exploration potential along A1/B1, the Harrison Fault, and Broach Lake.
The market may still be valuing JR as a single discovery.

The geology is increasingly pointing toward a multi-zone Athabasca uranium camp.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with F3 Uranium. Not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
Updates since then:

* Deepseek v4 is out. There *is* a 2-bit quant that can run within 90 GB ( https://t.co/yM1HMZXkXn ), and it works, however it's only fast on Apple hardware (I've head ~35 tok/s). On AMD, it's ~7 tok/s. IMO actually taking the effort to properly support more than one hardware manufacturer is a great example of the difference between mere "decentralized AI" and genuine "CROPS AI". I hope we can become better at this.
* https://t.co/CFYF1smBH3 also has alpha telegram support now. However, the path to adding your account is quite janky
* https://t.co/za4h233eYz looks promising as a way to run "dense" models (eg. Qwen 27B) more efficiently. It's janky, but on my 5090 laptop it seems to be ~2x more tok/s than llama.cpp
* VoxTerm (local AI recording, no third-party servers) continues to be developed https://t.co/GSdKzkD9Ql

And there's a lot more projects coming on the horizon.

One other thing that has been on my mind is that there's actually a lot of intersection between "CROPS ethereum access layer" and "CROPS AI". For example, we want a ZK way to make (paid) calls to remote LLMs. But if we have this, then it's just as useful for solving another problem: private RPC reads in Ethereum.

Another example: application-specific finetuned LLMs. Leanstral ( https://t.co/ilfww8ekJu ; I get ~38 tok/s on AMD) fits into < 70 GB, but can hold its own against 1T models on writing Lean code. Things like this are a huge boon for writing more secure code ( https://t.co/6YPWgVSzCg ). We should have models finetuned for Ethereum-related use cases as well.
